summary:
Histology in endometriotic lesions reflects cyclic estrogenic activity through characteristic findings like glandular-stromal structure and hemosiderin-laden macrophages resulting from recurrent bleeding due to estrogen-driven proliferation. While direct statements linking histology to high estrogen are absent, these pathological features arise from estrogen-responsive tissue dynamics during each menstrual cycle. 

(Note: This summary focuses only on clinically significant histopathological correlates related to hormonal influence.)  

Final Answer: \boxed{A}

Final Summary:  
Histology in endometriotic lesions shows patterns consistent with chronic estrogen stimulation—including glandular-stromal architecture and hemosiderin deposits—from recurring cyclic bleeds driven by ovarian steroid fluctuations. These features reflect underlying estrogen responsiveness even when not explicitly labeled “high estrogen,” making them representational of estrogen-mediated physiology in the context of endometriosis.
